title: On-Call Rotation and Escalation Policy Quiz
description: Test your skills designing on-call schedules, escalation paths, and alerting strategies that keep engineers sane and incidents short.

questions.title: Picking a Rotation Pattern
questions.description: A small distributed team is setting up its first on-call rotation.
questions.situation: You have six engineers across three time zones (US East, UK, India). Today, every alert goes to a shared Slack channel and whoever is awake responds. Pages are missed overnight. Leadership asks you to design the first formal rotation.
questions.codeExample:
Team layout:
- 2 engineers in New York (UTC-5)
- 2 engineers in London (UTC+0)
- 2 engineers in Bengaluru (UTC+5:30)

Current state:
- Single Slack channel for alerts
- 4 missed pages last month between 02:00-06:00 local for whoever was on-duty
- Average ack time at night: 38 minutes
- Average ack time during business hours: 4 minutes
options: One weekly rotation covering all 24 hours, primary picks one engineer regardless of location
A follow-the-sun rotation with three regional shifts so each engineer is only paged during their working hours
Keep the shared channel but require everyone to install the PagerDuty app on their phones
Rotate daily so nobody gets stuck with a bad week
questions.explanation: Follow-the-sun shifts let each region cover its waking hours. That fixes the root cause: people were being paged while asleep. Single 24-hour rotations create burnout fast for a six-person team. Pushing alerts to a shared channel without ownership is what got them into this mess. Daily rotation adds churn and breaks context between handoffs.
questions.hint: What problem are you actually solving? Read the ack time numbers again.

questions.title: When the Primary Doesn't Ack
questions.description: An alert fires at 03:17 and nobody acknowledges it. What should the escalation policy do next?
questions.situation: Your team just configured its first escalation policy in PagerDuty. The primary engineer's phone was on Do Not Disturb. The alert page below shows what happened. You're deciding what step 2 of the escalation should look like.
questions.codeExample:
Incident timeline:
03:17:02  PagerDuty triggers incident #4821 (severity: high)
03:17:03  Primary engineer (Alice) paged - SMS + push + phone call
03:17:04  No response
03:22:00  ??? what happens here ???

Current policy:
level_1:
  notify: alice@team.com
  timeout: 5m
level_2:
  notify: ???
  timeout: 5m
options: Re-page Alice every 5 minutes until she acknowledges, since she is the on-call
Send the alert to the team Slack channel and hope someone sees it
Page the secondary on-call engineer, then escalate to the engineering manager if still unacked after another 5 minutes
Auto-resolve the incident after 15 minutes since nobody is responding
questions.explanation: Escalation exists exactly for the case where the primary cannot respond, phone died, sleeping through it, sick, whatever. Hammering the same person rarely works. Slack channels are not a paging mechanism, nobody has Slack notifications on at 3am. Auto-resolving an unacked incident hides real outages. A proper policy moves to a secondary on a short timeout, then to a manager or wider team if the secondary also doesn't ack.
questions.hint: The point of escalation is to find someone who can actually respond, not to nag the same person harder.

questions.title: Reading the Signs of Alert Fatigue
questions.description: Your on-call engineers are starting to behave in concerning ways. Which signal points most clearly to alert fatigue?
questions.situation: You took over as the team's tech lead two months ago. You've noticed some patterns in how people interact with pages. The on-call retro this week included these comments.
questions.codeExample:
Retro notes:
- "I just ack and silence everything now, I'll check in the morning"
- "Half the alerts auto-resolve before I even open my laptop"
- 312 pages last month across 4 engineers (78 per person)
- 71% of pages auto-resolved without action
- 4 engineers asked to drop off the rotation this quarter
- One engineer admitted to muting PagerDuty during a shift
options: The team needs more training on how to use PagerDuty effectively
The number of pages and self-resolution rate prove the alerts are not actionable, and people are coping by ignoring them
Engineers are not committed enough to operational excellence
The on-call shifts are probably too short, longer shifts would build muscle memory
questions.explanation: When 71% of pages auto-resolve, the alert is not telling you anything useful, it is just noise. People who ignore or mute pages are not lazy, they are responding rationally to a broken signal. Training cannot fix bad alerts. Longer shifts make burnout worse, not better. The fix is to ruthlessly delete alerts that do not require human action and tune the rest.
questions.hint: Look at the auto-resolution rate. What does it tell you about the alerts themselves?

questions.title: Spot the Bug in This Escalation Policy
questions.description: Review this PagerDuty-style escalation policy. What's the most serious problem with it?
questions.situation: A teammate wrote this policy for a new service and asked for your review before they apply it. The service is customer-facing with a 99.9% availability SLO.
questions.codeExample:
name: payments-api-escalation
rules:
  - level: 1
    notify:
      - alice@team.com
    timeout_minutes: 30
  - level: 2
    notify:
      - bob@team.com
    timeout_minutes: 30
  - level: 3
    notify:
      - cto@company.com
    timeout_minutes: 60
repeat: false
options: The CTO should not be on an escalation policy, only engineers should
The 30 minute timeout is far too long for a customer-facing service; the SLO allows about 43 minutes of total downtime per month
The policy should use phone calls instead of email notifications
Three escalation levels is too many, two is the standard
questions.explanation: A 99.9% SLO leaves roughly 43 minutes of error budget per month. Waiting 30 minutes before even paging a secondary burns most of your monthly budget on a single incident. For a customer-facing service, level 1 timeouts of 5 to 10 minutes are typical. Notification channel (the policy already includes the engineer's contact, channels are configured per-user), having a leader as a final fallback, and the number of levels are all reasonable choices.
questions.hint: Convert 99.9% availability into minutes of allowed downtime per month, then look at the timeout values.

questions.title: Symptom-Based vs Cause-Based Alerts
questions.description: You have two Prometheus alert rules covering the same service. Which one should you keep?
questions.situation: The on-call team is auditing alerts on the checkout-service. They want to keep one of these two rules and delete the other. The service has an SLO of 99.5% successful requests.
questions.codeExample:
# Rule A
- alert: CheckoutHighCPU
  expr: rate(node_cpu_seconds_total{job="checkout",mode!="idle"}[5m]) > 0.85
  for: 10m
  labels:
    severity: page
  annotations:
    summary: "Checkout service CPU above 85%"

# Rule B
- alert: CheckoutErrorRateHigh
  expr: |
    sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="checkout",status=~"5.."}[5m]))
    / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="checkout"}[5m])) > 0.005
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: page
  annotations:
    summary: "Checkout 5xx rate exceeds SLO budget"
options: Rule A, because high CPU is a leading indicator and you want to catch problems before users see them
Rule B, because it pages on actual user-facing symptoms tied directly to the SLO
Keep both, since CPU and error rate are independent signals
Delete both and use synthetic monitoring instead
questions.explanation: Page on what users feel, not on causes. High CPU is often fine, a service can run hot and still serve requests perfectly. Paging on CPU produces noise and trains the team to ignore alerts. Rule B fires when actual users are getting errors, which is what your SLO measures. Keep cause-based metrics for dashboards and post-incident analysis, not for waking people up.
questions.hint: Which of these alerts can fire while everything users care about is working fine?

questions.title: Choosing the Right Severity
questions.description: Pick the correct severity for the scenario below using the team's severity matrix.
questions.situation: Your team uses a four-level severity matrix. A new alert just fired and you need to triage. The internal admin dashboard is returning 500 errors. Only 5 engineering staff use this dashboard, never customers. It's used to view sales reports.
questions.codeExample:
Severity matrix:
SEV-1: Customer-facing outage or data loss. Page primary + manager. All hands.
SEV-2: Major feature degraded for customers, or full outage of an internal critical tool. Page primary.
SEV-3: Minor customer impact, or internal tool degraded. Ticket only, fix during business hours.
SEV-4: No impact, informational. No page, log only.

Incident details:
- Service: internal-admin-dashboard
- Symptom: HTTP 500 on /reports endpoint
- Users affected: 5 internal staff
- Workaround: query the warehouse directly
- Time: 02:14 local
options: SEV-1, any 500 error indicates a serious problem that needs all-hands attention
SEV-2, the dashboard is broken and internal tools need to stay up
SEV-3, internal tool with a workaround and no customer impact, fix it in the morning
SEV-4, since no customer is affected this is just informational
questions.explanation: Severity should reflect impact, not the size of the error message. No customer is affected, a workaround exists, and only five internal users use the tool. Waking someone up for this trains them to ignore SEV-1s. SEV-1 is reserved for customer-facing pain or data loss. SEV-2 is for major internal tools that block work, a reports page does not qualify. SEV-4 understates it, the dashboard genuinely is broken and needs a ticket and a fix.
questions.hint: Look at impact and whether a workaround exists. Severity is not about how scary the error looks.

questions.title: What to Include in a Handoff
questions.description: You're ending your week-long shift. What belongs in the handoff to the next on-call?
questions.situation: You spent the week on-call. A few incidents are still unresolved, you noticed a noisy alert, and there's a planned database migration tomorrow morning. You have 15 minutes to write the handoff before logging off.
questions.codeExample:
This week's state:
- Open incident #5102: intermittent 502s on cart-service, root cause not found, mitigation: retries enabled
- Alert "DiskUsage85" fired 14 times, every time it cleared itself within 10 minutes
- Planned: db schema migration scheduled for Tuesday 09:00 by data team
- New runbook added for Redis evictions
- Two engineers in EU region taking PTO Mon-Wed
options: A summary of what you fixed this week, since the next on-call needs to know your accomplishments
Open incidents, ongoing investigations, known noisy alerts, planned changes, and any unusual coverage gaps
Just the open incidents, everything else can be found in PagerDuty history
A full retrospective with timelines for every incident you handled
questions.explanation: A good handoff transfers the operational context the next person needs to act fast. Open incidents tell them what is still broken. Noisy alerts let them filter signal from noise. Planned changes prevent them from being surprised by a database migration at 9am. Coverage gaps matter when they need to escalate. What you fixed this week and full retrospectives belong in different documents, the handoff is about what the next person needs in the next 7 days.
questions.hint: Think about what the next on-call needs to do their job, not what you want them to know about yours.

questions.title: Multi-Window Burn Rate Alert
questions.description: Why does this SLO alert use two windows instead of one?
questions.situation: Your team is moving away from threshold-based alerts and toward SLO burn rate alerts. A senior SRE proposes this rule for the payments service with a 99.9% availability SLO over 30 days.
questions.codeExample:
- alert: PaymentsErrorBudgetBurn
  expr: |
    (
      sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payments",status=~"5.."}[1h]))
      / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payments"}[1h])) > (14.4 * 0.001)
    )
    and
    (
      sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payments",status=~"5.."}[5m]))
      / sum(rate(http_requests_total{job="payments"}[5m])) > (14.4 * 0.001)
    )
  for: 2m
  labels:
    severity: page
options: Two windows just doubles the alert evaluations, it's a mistake
The long window detects sustained burn and the short window makes the alert fire quickly without waiting an hour
The 5 minute window is for tests, it should be removed in production
Two windows let you alert on both the average and the median error rate
questions.explanation: Multi-window multi-burn-rate alerts solve the trade-off between alert latency and false positives. A short window alone fires on every small blip. A long window alone takes too long to detect a real outage. Requiring both to exceed the burn rate threshold gives you fast detection (5m window) while filtering out brief spikes (1h window must also be burning). This is the pattern described in the Google SRE Workbook for SLO-based alerting.
questions.hint: What would happen if you only used the 5-minute window? What about only the 1-hour window?

questions.title: Fixing a Noisy Disk Alert
questions.description: This alert paged seven times last week. How do you fix it?
questions.situation: Every time a nightly batch job runs, this alert fires for about 20 minutes while temporary files are written. Then the job cleans up and the alert resolves. The on-call has paged seven times in the last week from this single rule.
questions.codeExample:
- alert: HostDiskUsageHigh
  expr: (1 - node_filesystem_avail_bytes / node_filesystem_size_bytes) > 0.80
  for: 1m
  labels:
    severity: page
  annotations:
    summary: "Disk usage above 80% on {{ $labels.instance }}"

Context:
- Disk reaches 82-86% during nightly batch job 02:00-02:25
- Recovers to 60% after job finishes
- Job runs every night, has run reliably for 18 months
- True disk-full incident in past 18 months: 0
options: Raise the threshold to 90% so the batch job no longer triggers it
Change the alert to predictive: page only if disk is filling at a rate that would hit 100% within 4 hours, and ticket (not page) at static high thresholds
Disable the alert during the 02:00-02:25 window using a silence
Add more disk space to the host so it never crosses 80%
questions.explanation: Static thresholds on disk usage are almost always wrong. Raising the threshold to 90% just delays the problem and hides actual leaks. Silences need to be maintained and break when the batch job moves. Adding disk treats the symptom not the design flaw. A predictive alert based on fill rate catches actual problems (slow leaks, runaway logs) without firing on planned, self-correcting spikes. Pair it with a ticket-level static alert for the unusual case of a sudden jump.
questions.hint: The alert fires every night on a known, healthy pattern. What does that tell you about the threshold model?

questions.title: Making On-Call Sustainable
questions.description: Your best engineer just asked to leave the rotation. What's the most effective long-term fix?
questions.situation: Three engineers have left the rotation in the past six months. The remaining engineers are picking up more shifts. One of them, your strongest IC, just sent this Slack message.
questions.codeExample:
Slack message from senior engineer:

"Hey, I need to talk about on-call. Last month I got paged 23 times,
11 of those were between midnight and 5am. I haven't had a full
weekend off in 7 weeks because the rotation is short-staffed.
I know we need coverage but I can't keep doing this. I'm thinking
about looking for another job if this doesn't change."

Current state:
- 4 engineers in rotation, was 7 six months ago
- Average pages per week per engineer: 6
- Page volume trend: increasing
- Compensation: none, on-call is unpaid
options: Add on-call pay so people are compensated for the pain
Hire more engineers to spread the rotation across more people
Treat the page volume as a bug: dedicate one sprint per quarter to alert quality and operational debt, reduce noise at the source
Move to a 24-hour shift model so engineers only do one bad day at a time
questions.explanation: Compensation, more headcount, and shift restructuring all treat symptoms. The actual problem is 23 pages a month, most of them at night. If those pages were actionable and valuable, the engineer would feel useful. The real fix is to reduce page volume at the source: delete useless alerts, fix the bugs causing repeat incidents, raise thresholds, automate runbook actions. Comp helps morale, but if every comped on-call shift is still a sleepless night, retention will not improve. The engineer in this story is asking for fewer 3am wake-ups, not a bigger paycheck for them.
questions.hint: Look at the page volume numbers. What if those numbers were cut by 80%?

questions.title: Routing Alerts to the Right Team
questions.description: An alert for a database is paging the application team. How should routing be set up?
questions.situation: Your app team has been paged five times this month for issues that turned out to be database problems. Each time, the app on-call had to wake up, investigate, then page the database team. The database team has its own on-call rotation but no alerts are routed to them directly.
questions.codeExample:
Current routing (Alertmanager):
route:
  receiver: app-team-pager
  group_by: ['alertname']

receivers:
  - name: app-team-pager
    pagerduty_configs:
      - service_key: <app-team-key>

Recent incidents app-team handled:
- DB connection pool exhausted (real cause: long-running query)
- Replication lag spike (real cause: backup job)
- Disk full on db primary (real cause: log rotation broken)
All routed to app-team, then manually escalated to db-team.
options: Page both teams every time so nobody has to escalate manually
Route alerts to the team that owns the underlying service, using labels like service or team to pick the receiver
Have the app team learn database administration so they can fix these issues themselves
Move all database alerts to email so they don't wake anyone at night
questions.explanation: Alerts should reach the team that owns the failing component. Routing by labels (service, team, component) sends each alert to the right rotation directly. Paging both teams creates duplicate work and dilutes ownership, the database team will assume the app team is on it and vice versa. Cross-training is good but does not fix the routing bug. Moving database alerts to email loses urgency for genuinely critical issues. The fix is at the routing layer: tag metrics with owning team, route by label.
questions.hint: Who can actually fix a database issue at 3am? Make sure the alert reaches them directly.

questions.title: Handling Mid-Shift Coverage Gaps
questions.description: Your on-call gets sick mid-shift and a public holiday is approaching. How do you handle coverage?
questions.situation: It's Tuesday afternoon. Alice is on-call for the week (Mon-Sun). She just messaged that she's running a fever and going to sleep, she'll be out for 24 to 48 hours. Friday is a US national holiday and three of your six engineers are US-based. Schedule below.
questions.codeExample:
This week's schedule:
Mon  Tue  Wed  Thu  Fri  Sat  Sun
Alice Alice Alice Alice Alice Alice Alice

Secondary: Bob (US, off Friday for holiday)

Team availability:
- Alice (UK): Out sick 24-48h
- Bob (US): Available now, off Friday
- Carol (US): Off Friday
- Dave (US): Off Friday
- Eve (India): Available
- Frank (India): Available

It is currently 14:00 UTC Tuesday.
options: Move Alice's whole week to Bob and tell Bob to find someone for Friday himself
Use PagerDuty overrides: assign Bob primary until Alice returns, then split Friday between Eve and Frank by time zone; document the change so everyone knows who to escalate to
Leave the schedule as-is and hope Alice recovers before any alerts fire
Send the alerts to a shared Slack channel for the next few days until things stabilize
questions.explanation: Coverage gaps are normal and need an explicit process. Time-bounded overrides in your paging tool make the new assignments visible to everyone, including monitoring and escalations. Splitting Friday between two India-based engineers gives the US team a real day off without leaving the rotation uncovered. Pushing the problem onto Bob without a plan creates ambiguity and risks missed pages, particularly on Friday. Doing nothing or falling back to a shared Slack channel are exactly the patterns a proper escalation policy is supposed to replace.
questions.hint: You have engineers awake in India when the US holiday hits. Use the time zones you have.

questions.title: Using Postmortems to Improve On-Call
questions.description: How should postmortem findings feed back into the on-call program?
questions.situation: After a SEV-2 incident last week, the postmortem produced a list of action items. Your manager asks you which of these actually improves the on-call program long-term rather than just patching this one incident.
questions.codeExample:
Postmortem action items:
1. Add a runbook entry for "queue consumer stalled"
2. Delete the alert "QueueDepthAbove1000" (fired all the time, ignored)
3. Replace it with "QueueConsumerLagAbove5m" (symptom-based)
4. Add automated restart for stalled consumers via systemd
5. Schedule a quarterly review of alerts that paged but had no action taken
6. Buy the team t-shirts that say "I survived incident #6042"
7. Train two more engineers on the queue subsystem so the rotation is not bottlenecked
options: Items 1 and 6, since runbooks and morale matter most
Just item 4, automation is the only real fix
Items 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 7 all reduce future page load or speed up response; item 6 is fine but irrelevant to the on-call program
Items 2 and 3 only, since deleting noisy alerts is the single highest-impact action
questions.explanation: A good postmortem produces operational improvements across multiple layers: documentation (runbooks), signal quality (delete noisy alerts, add symptom-based ones), automation (self-heal where possible), process (regular review of useless pages), and people (spread knowledge). Item 6 is fine for morale but does not change on-call load. Picking only one category misses the compound effect, the rotation gets better when you reduce noise AND automate fixes AND share knowledge. Treat the action item list as a portfolio of improvements, not a single silver bullet.
questions.hint: Which items will reduce the number of pages, the time to fix, or the people-bottleneck? Probably more than you think.
